seo.pageTitle: Jeju Southern UNESCO Spots Day Tour | AtoC Korea
seo.metaDescription: 9-hour southern Jeju small-group loop: Eoseungsaengak Trail (Hallasan UNESCO), Camellia Hill, Cheonjeyeon Falls, Jusangjeolli Cliff, Osulloc tea. 4.9/5 rated.
seo.title: Jeju Southern UNESCO Spots Day Tour · 9-Hour Small-Group · All Admissions Included

catalog_card.title: Jeju Southern Top UNESCO Spots Day Tour
catalog_card.priceLabel: From US$38.70 per person (10% off, was $43)
catalog_card.shortCardDescription: A small-group day tour through southern Jeju's signature stops — the Eoseungsaengak Trail along Hallasan (UNESCO biosphere), Camellia Hill garden, the three-tier Cheonjeyeon Waterfall, the hexagonal Jusangjeolli Cliff, and the O'sulloc Tea Museum. Four pickup points across Jeju City and the airport, certified English/Chinese-speaking guide, all admissions included, no shopping pressure. The most reviewed entry-level Jeju tour at 4.9/5 across 648 reviews.
catalog_card.subtitle: South Jeju UNESCO loop — Hallasan trail, waterfalls, Jusangjeolli columnar cliff.

headlineLine1: Hallasan to the columnar cliffs, waterfall to tea fields —
headlineLine2: southern Jeju's signature loop in a value-priced day

itineraryStops.name: Pickup (4 points across Jeju City)
itineraryStops.description: **Pickup is consolidated at four central Jeju City points** to minimize morning crisscrossing: **Jeju International Airport (제주공항)** for guests with same-day arrivals; **Jeju City Hall (제주시청)** for the Yeon-dong / Nohyeong / Old Town hotel cluster; **Hagwi-dong (하귀동)** as the westernmost pickup before heading south; and **Dongmun Traditional Market (동문시장)** as the optional eastern Jeju City pickup near Tapdong waterfront. Sequence is Hagwi → Airport → City Hall → Dongmun → southbound Route 1100 toward Hallasan. The minicoach is **small-group (max ~14 pax)** with English-speaking guide. **Bilingual printed itinerary card** distributed at pickup, weather brief at first stop. **Route 1100 closure check** is performed before departure — if closed for snow/ice, the Hallasan stop substitutes to a lower-elevation alternate (typically Mysterious Road or Jeju National Museum).
highlights: **4 pickup points** — Airport, City Hall, Hagwi, Dongmun
Sequence west→east minimizes morning coach distance
**Small-group minicoach (max 14 pax)** with English guide
Bilingual itinerary card + weather brief at first stop
**Route 1100 closure check** before departure (winter snow contingency)
Total morning transit Jeju City → Hallasan trailhead: ~50 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Four pickup points cover Jeju City's hotel + transport zones (airport, mid-city, old town, market district) without forcing guests across the city. Sequencing west→east lands the coach at Hallasan ~10:20 — early enough to catch the morning Eoseungsaengak trail before midday haze + crowds. **Route 1100 closure protocol** ensures guests are informed about winter contingency before departure — Jeju's only highest-altitude national highway is closed ~5–10 days/year for ice.

itineraryStops.name: Eoseungsaengak Trail (Hallasan UNESCO)
itineraryStops.category: UNESCO Volcanic Geology — Hallasan Lower Trail (Triple Natural designation)
itineraryStops.description: **Eoseungsaengak (어승생악, 1,169 m)** is a parasitic cone (오름 / *oreum*) on the western flank of **Hallasan (한라산, 1,950 m)** — Korea's highest peak and the geological heart of Jeju. Hallasan itself carries an **unparalleled designation stack**: **UNESCO World Natural Heritage (inscribed 2007-07-02)**, **UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (2002)**, **Korea National Park (designated 1970-03-24)**, and **Natural Monument No. 182 (1966)** — making it the only Korean site holding all three UNESCO natural designations simultaneously. The Eoseungsaengak trailhead at **Eorimok Visitor Center** climbs **only ~200 m over 1.3 km** to a wide summit deck — a 60-minute round trip that delivers the **'Hallasan UNESCO taste'** without the full 9-hour Baengnokdam crater ascent. From the deck on clear days you see the **Yellow Sea, Hallasan's Baengnokdam summit caldera (백록담), and the parasitic cone field** stretching south. Botanical highlight: this elevation band (1,000–1,300 m) is the **transition zone between deciduous broadleaf forest and the Korean fir (구상나무, *Abies koreana* — IUCN Near Threatened, endemic to South Korean high mountains) belt** above. Hallasan harbors **2,000+ plant species** total — roughly **half of all plants on the Korean Peninsula**. A historical footnote sits at the summit deck: a **1945 Imperial Japanese tochika (토치카, concrete pillbox/communication post)** built in the war's final months — one of the highest surviving WWII Japanese fortifications in Korea. Admission and parking are **free**; **no reservation required** for Eoseungsaengak (unlike Baengnokdam summit which caps daily climbers at 1,000).
highlights: **Hallasan = only Korean site with Triple Natural designation**: UNESCO World Heritage 2007 + Biosphere 2002 + National Park 1970
**Eoseungsaengak summit 1,169 m** — parasitic cone (oreum), one of 360+ on Jeju
Trail length **1.3 km one-way / ~200 m gain** — wide steps + boardwalk, doable in athletic shoes
Deck panorama covers **Hallasan's Baengnokdam crater, Yellow Sea, and southern oreum field**
Botanical transition zone — **Korean fir (*Abies koreana*, IUCN Near Threatened)** belt begins ~100 m above
Summit holds **1945 Imperial Japanese tochika pillbox** — a rare high-altitude WWII relic
**FREE admission** + free parking; no reservation cap (unlike Baengnokdam summit, capped at 1,000/day)
Hallasan harbors **2,000+ plant species** — half of all flora on the Korean Peninsula
Round-trip dwell **~60 minutes** = full 'UNESCO taste' without 9-hour Baengnokdam ascent
timeUsed: Eorimok parking + restroom + trail brief — ~10 min
Summit deck + photos + tochika viewing — ~15 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **UNESCO opening anchor of the south-Jeju route**. Where the highlights-loop tour (14a Tour 1) places Eoseungsaengak as a first-time-Jeju cone-climbing primer, this south-UNESCO route uses it as the **first of the day's UNESCO-grade stops**, explicitly framed as 'Korea's only Triple Natural designation site' (UNESCO World Heritage 2007 + Biosphere 2002 + National Park 1970 + Natural Monument 1966). The morning placement (10:20 arrival) catches the **panoramic clarity before midday haze** typical of Hallasan's mid-elevation slopes. After Eoseungsaengak the coach drops to lower elevations through the southwestern volcanic plateau, threading the day's other UNESCO + Geopark anchors (Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon, Osulloc tea fields).

sources: UNESCO WHC inscription 1264 (Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes, 2007-07-02)
UNESCO Biosphere Reserve — Jeju Island (2002 designation)
Hallasan National Park Administration — Eoseungsaengak trail official
Cultural Heritage Administration — Hallasan Natural Monument 1966
Korea National Park Service — Hallasan visitor management records

itineraryStops.name: Camellia Hill (year-round signature flower garden)
itineraryStops.category: Botanical Garden — Largest Camellia Arboretum in the East
itineraryStops.description: **Camellia Hill (카멜리아힐, Jeju Camellia Arboretum)** is a **172,000 m² botanical garden** in Andeok, **the largest camellia arboretum in the East** by acreage and species count: **6,000 camellia trees representing over 500 species sourced from 80 countries**, accumulated over **30+ years** of curation by founder Yang Jong-mun. The garden's signature is its **year-round flowering calendar**, deliberately engineered so that visiting in any season produces a different color palette: **November–March = camellia peak** (red, pink, white, cream-beige varieties — the garden's namesake season); **March–May = cherry blossoms + tulips + late camellias** layered together in dense color mass; **June–August = hydrangeas** (both outdoor stands and a climate-controlled greenhouse showing year-round display); **September–October = fall foliage + late-season bloomers**. Walking paths thread through themed sections — **European Garden, Korean Native Forest Section, Cherry Blossom Walk, Hydrangea Field** — connected by a winding 2.5-km loop that takes ~60–75 min to complete at a leisurely pace. Notable architectural moment: a **glass greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex at the entrance, which serves as both indoor weather refuge (the only ~15% indoor portion of the visit) and a tea/coffee dwell space with garden views. **Drama filming credit**: variety show **'EXchange / Transit Love 3' (환승연애)** featured Camellia Hill as the location of Ju-won and Yu-jeong's memorable date scene — a draw for fans of the K-reality genre. **Open 365 days a year**; **no outdoor lighting** means the garden closes at sundown (winter ~17:00, summer ~19:00). Admission: **Adult ₩12,000 / Youth + Senior ₩10,000 / Child (4–11) ₩9,000 / FREE under 3**.
highlights: **172,000 m²** — largest camellia arboretum in the East
**6,000 trees / 500+ species from 80 countries** — accumulated 30+ years
**Year-round bloom calendar** — Nov–Mar camellias, Mar–May cherry + tulip, Jun–Aug hydrangea
**Themed sections**: European Garden, Korean Native Forest, Cherry Walk, Hydrangea Field
Glass **greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex — indoor refuge (15% of visit)
**'EXchange / Transit Love 3'** filming location — Ju-won & Yu-jeong's date scene
**Open 365 days**; closes at sundown (no outdoor lighting)
Admission **₩12,000 adult / ₩10,000 youth+senior / ₩9,000 child (4–11) / FREE under 3**
**~85% outdoor** garden walking — comfortable shoes recommended
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Botanical mid-day anchor** — and the day's primary 'softer' counterpoint to the morning UNESCO geology + afternoon waterfall + columnar-jointing weight. Camellia Hill sits **inland between Hallasan and the south coast**, perfectly placed as a 11:55 arrival (just before lunch), filling the geographic gap between Eoseungsaengak (on Hallasan's western flank) and Cheonjeyeon (on the south coast). Compared with **Spirited Garden (Bunjae Yetdo, smaller, ₩12,000, bonsai-focused)** or **Hallim Park (300,000 m², ₩15,000, more diffuse — botanical + folk village + caves combined)**, Camellia Hill is **species-deepest, year-round-blooming, and architecturally curated** — the highest yield-per-minute botanical stop in southwest Jeju. **Year-round bloom calendar** guarantees the stop's photographic value regardless of season, removing the seasonal-risk that plagues Hallim Park (winter) or hydrangea-only gardens (summer-locked).

sources: Korea Tourism Organization — Top Korean botanical gardens registry
Kakao TV / Wavve programming archive — 'EXchange / Transit Love 3' filming locations

itineraryStops.name: Lunch — South Jeju Local
itineraryStops.category: Midday Reset — Seogwipo / Andeok Local Cuisine
itineraryStops.description: **60-minute reset between Camellia Hill and Cheonjeyeon Falls** — the south Jeju coast is one of Korea's premier seafood landing zones, and the Andeok-Seogwipo corridor sits 10–15 min from the next stop. Default rotation: **galchi-jorim (갈치조림, braised cutlassfish)** — Jeju is Korea's primary cutlassfish landing, with the south coast delivering the freshest catches; **mulhoe (물회, cold raw-fish soup)** in summer (June–September); or **heuk-doeji gui (흑돼지구이, Jeju black pig BBQ)** year-round (heritage breed designated **Cultural Heritage Material No. 5, 2015**). **Vegetarian alternative**: bibim-guksu with seasonal Jeju vegetables. **Halal-friendly options** available at one venue in the rotation — disclose at pickup. Lunch venue is **10-min coach to Cheonjeyeon Falls** entrance.
highlights: **South Jeju seafood corridor** — Andeok–Seogwipo coastal restaurant strip
**Galchi-jorim (갈치조림)** — Jeju is Korea's primary cutlassfish landing
**Mulhoe (물회)** in summer; **heuk-doeji** year-round option
Black pig is **Cultural Heritage Material No. 5 (2015)**
**Vegetarian + halal-friendly** options on request (disclose at pickup)
**10-min coach to Cheonjeyeon Falls** — minimal post-lunch transit
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: South-coast positioning means **10-min hop to Cheonjeyeon Falls** afterward — preserves photo-window timing. Galchi is also a south-Jeju regional specialty (Jeju is Korea's primary cutlassfish landing port), so the geographic placement adds culinary identity to the lunch slot. Pace also breaks the morning's Hallasan + botanical garden walking before the afternoon's three remaining UNESCO/heritage anchors.

itineraryStops.name: Cheonjeyeon Waterfall (Pond of the Gods)
itineraryStops.description: **Cheonjeyeon Falls (천제연, '연 = pond + 천제 = Emperor of Heaven')** is a **three-tier waterfall** in Jungmun's volcanic gorge, designated **Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** for its rare ecological combination. The name comes from a **Joseon-era Daoist legend that seven nymphs (선녀, *seonnyeo*) descended from heaven nightly to bathe in the upper pond** — a myth reinforced by the dramatic site's microclimate of perpetual mist. The **1st tier (22 m fall, 21 m deep pond)** drops into a circular basalt cauldron and only flows after heavy rain — most days the upper pond sits glassy and reflective. The **2nd tier (30 m fall) flows year-round** — the dominant photographic subject. The 3rd tier completes the descent to the sea. The **120 m / 78 m-high Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교, 'Bridge of the Seven Nymphs')** crosses the gorge midway through the loop — its cast-bronze relief panels along both sides depict **all seven nymphs in flight**, a literal architectural realization of the founding legend. Surrounding forest is a **rare-plant habitat** including **Korean toadlily (***Tricyrtis macropoda***)** and the endangered **Mankyua chejuense fern** (Jeju endemic, IUCN Critically Endangered). Loop length: **0.8 km, ~30 min walk** with several steep stair sections. Admission: **Adult ₩2,500 / Youth ₩1,350 / FREE under 7 + over 65**. Cheonjeyeon is also a member of the **'Yeongjusipgyeong (영주십경, 10 Scenic Wonders of Jeju)'** Joseon-era literati canon — alongside Hallasan summit, Seongsan Sunrise, and Jeongbang Falls — meaning today's southwest loop covers **2 of the 10 (Hallasan + Cheonjeyeon)** in a single day. The biennial **Jeju Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival** held at the bridge in May (every odd year) reenacts the descent legend with traditional dance and musical performance — reservations required when the festival coincides with a tour date. Hours **09:00–18:00** year-round (last entry 17:30).
highlights: **Natural Monument No. 378 (designated 1993)** — three-tier waterfall + rare-plant habitat
Name **'Cheonjeyeon' = 'Pond of the Emperor of Heaven'** — Joseon Daoist legend
**Seven Nymphs (선녀) legend** carved into the **120 m × 78 m Seonimgyo Bridge** relief panels
**1st tier (22 m / 21 m pond)** — only flows after heavy rain; **2nd tier (30 m) flows year-round**
Habitat for **Mankyua chejuense fern** — Jeju endemic, IUCN Critically Endangered
**0.8 km loop / ~30 min** — moderate stairs, footwear matters
Admission **₩2,500 adult / ₩1,350 youth / FREE under 7 + over 65**
**Jeju Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival** held biennially at the site — bridge ceremony
**Misty microclimate** — natural cooling, popular summer afternoon retreat
timeUsed: Descent to 1st tier viewing platform — ~10 min
Seonimgyo Bridge + nymph relief panels — ~15 min
Forest loop + ascent back to coach — ~25 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Mid-afternoon Natural Monument anchor** of the south-UNESCO route. Where the southwest-Hallasan-Osulloc-Aewol sister tour (14a Tour 2) frames Cheonjeyeon as a Daoist-mythology + Mankyua chejuense fern habitat anchor, this south-UNESCO route uses it as the day's **second of three explicit Natural Monument anchors** (after Hallasan, before Jusangjeolli). The 14:30 arrival catches **soft afternoon light through the canyon walls**, ideal for the Seonimgyo Bridge cast-bronze relief panels. Geographic placement: Cheonjeyeon sits **5 min from Jusangjeolli** along the south coast — the natural late-afternoon sequencing.

sources: Cultural Heritage Administration — Natural Monument No. 378 designation
Jeju Special Self-Governing Province — Seonimgyo Bridge construction records
Korea Plant Society — Mankyua chejuense IUCN Critically Endangered listing
Jeju Folklore Society — Daoist seven-nymph legend documentation

itineraryStops.name: Jusangjeolli Cliff (Daepo Columnar Joints)
itineraryStops.description: **Daepo Jusangjeolli (대포 주상절리, 'Jisatgae')** is a **1 km stretch of hexagonal columnar-jointed basalt cliff** on the Jungmun coast — designated **Natural Monument No. 443 on 2005-01-06**. The columns rise **30–40 m** from the sea, packed in tightly fitted **5- to 7-sided polygons** (mostly hexagons) — a geometry produced when **molten basaltic lava from a Hallasan flank eruption (~250,000–140,000 years ago) reached the ocean and contracted as it cooled**, fracturing perpendicular to the cooling surface. Globally, columnar jointing is best known from **Giant's Causeway (UK)**, **Devils Postpile (US)**, and **Fingal's Cave (Scotland)** — Jusangjeolli is **East Asia's premier example** and the most accessible. The 800 m **boardwalk loop** delivers visitors to a railed cliff edge directly above the columns; **on high-tide days waves break against the columns and throw spray up to 20 m**, occasionally over the rail. Admission: **Adult ₩2,000 / Youth ₩1,000 / FREE under 7 and over 65**. The site is part of the **Jeju Global Geopark (UNESCO designated 2010-10)** — one of **13 official geosites** on the island, alongside Seongsan Ilchulbong, Manjanggul Lava Tube, and Suwolbong. The clifftop deck is **barrier-free** (wheelchair/stroller accessible). Three viewing platforms along the loop give different column angles: the **first platform** shows the columns in profile (best for the hexagonal-geometry shot), the **mid-platform** looks straight down for top-of-column patterns, and the **far platform** captures the 1 km coastal sweep with Hallasan as backdrop on clear days. **Photographic peak window: 14:00–16:00**, when westering sunlight lights the column faces frontally; sunset visits bring silhouette compositions but lose column-detail texture. Hours: **09:00–18:00 (last entry 17:30)**, year-round operation.
highlights: **Natural Monument No. 443** — designated **2005-01-06**, the most recent Jeju coastal NM
Columns rise **30–40 m**, packed in **5- to 7-sided polygons** (mostly hexagons) along **1 km** of coastline
Formation: **Hallasan flank lava cooled in the sea ~250,000–140,000 years ago**, contracting perpendicular to cooling face
**East Asia's premier columnar-jointing site** — peer to Giant's Causeway, Devils Postpile, Fingal's Cave
**Spray up to 20 m on high-tide days** — wave-fountain photography window
**0.8 km boardwalk loop**, ~15 min walk — barrier-free deck, wheelchair/stroller accessible
Part of **Jeju Global Geopark (UNESCO 2010-10)** — 13 island-wide geosites
Admission **₩2,000 adult / ₩1,000 youth / FREE under 7 + over 65**
Hallasan summit visible to the north on clear days, **directly downstream** geologically
timeUsed: Walk to first viewing platform — ~5 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Day's third Natural Monument anchor** + **the catalog's premier columnar-jointing geological showcase**. Where the highlights-loop tour (14a Tour 1) frames Jusangjeolli as a Hallasan downstream lava-flow signature, this south-UNESCO route adds it as the **closing geological climax of the day's UNESCO arc** (Hallasan → Cheonjeyeon → Jusangjeolli). The 15:40 arrival catches **late-afternoon golden light on the 30–40 m basalt columns** — the most photographed lighting window. Compared with global columnar-jointing peers (Giant's Causeway in Northern Ireland, Devils Postpile in California, Fingal's Cave in Scotland), Jusangjeolli is **East Asia's premier site** and accessibly walkable in a 15-minute loop, making it viable as one of multiple stops in a single day.

sources: Cultural Heritage Administration — Natural Monument No. 443 designation (2005-01-06)
Jeju UNESCO Global Geopark — Daepo Jusangjeolli geosite documentation
Korean Society of Petroleum Engineers — columnar-jointing geology records
Korea Geological Survey — Jeju volcanic flow chronology

itineraryStops.name: O'sulloc Tea Museum + Green Tea Fields
itineraryStops.category: Tea Heritage Museum — Korea's Largest Green Tea Field (Seogwang Dawon)
itineraryStops.description: **O'Sulloc Tea Museum (오설록 티 뮤지엄, '오설록' meaning 'meeting snow + green')** is the tea-culture flagship of **Amorepacific Corporation** — Korea's largest beauty company. Founder **Suh Sung-whan personally pioneered the project in 1979**, reclaiming volcanic ash farmland on Jeju's Seogwang plateau when conventional wisdom held tea couldn't be grown commercially in Korea. Today the **Seogwang Dawon plantation (서광다원)** is **Korea's largest green-tea field**, producing **~700 tonnes annually** across Amorepacific's three Jeju estates. The museum building **opened 2001** in the plantation's heart and was named to **designboom's Top 10 art museums** for its minimalist green-roofed pavilion-amid-tea-rows design. **FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~**2 million annual visitors**. The visit splits naturally into three segments: **(1) Museum interior** — tea production process, antique tea-ware collection from Tang dynasty (618–907 CE) onward, and rooftop observation deck with panoramic plantation view; **(2) Tea House cafe** — signature **green tea rolling cake (오설록 롤케이크)** and **green tea ice cream** served from the tea-leaf harvest year directly; **(3) Plantation walking paths** — gravel rows between tea bushes, popular for sunset photography at the western edge. **Innisfree Jeju House** (Amorepacific's natural cosmetics brand) is a **5-minute walk** for guests interested in the broader brand ecosystem — also free admission with optional tangerine soap DIY workshop (₩15,000). The museum building itself, designed by Korean architect **Mass Studies (Cho Min-suk)**, takes a deliberately low-profile horizontal form that mirrors the tea-row geometry rather than competing with the agricultural landscape — the architectural deference earning it both the designboom Top 10 listing and ongoing use as a case study in Korean contemporary museum design. Optional add-on: the **Osulloc Tea Class (₩30,000 per person, 60 min, advance booking required)** — guided tea-flight tasting with brewing technique demonstration on Korean ceramics, available daily **10:00–16:00**, English-speaking instruction on Wed/Sat sessions.
highlights: **Amorepacific** flagship — Korea's largest beauty company; founder Suh Sung-whan pioneered Jeju tea **1979**
**Seogwang Dawon = Korea's largest green-tea field**; ~700 tonnes annual production
Museum **opened 2001** — designboom Top 10 art museums (architecture)
**FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~2 million annual visitors
Antique tea-ware collection spanning **Tang dynasty (618–907 CE)** to modern Korean Joseon ceramics
**Green tea rolling cake** + **green tea ice cream** = signature cafe items
**Innisfree Jeju House** 5-min walk — free, optional tangerine soap DIY ₩15,000
Plantation walking paths **best at golden hour (16:00–17:00)** for photography
Rooftop deck + ground-level rows give two distinct elevations for tea-row composition
timeUsed: Tea House cafe (rolling cake + ice cream) — ~20 min
Optional Innisfree Jeju House visit — ~10 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Cultural-heritage finale of the south-UNESCO route**. Where the southwest-Hallasan sister tour (14a Tour 2) places Osulloc as a mid-afternoon tea-culture anchor, this south-UNESCO route uses it as the **day's final cultural heritage layer** — a deliberate softening contrast to the morning's geological intensity. Osulloc completes the UNESCO-and-heritage narrative arc: Hallasan (UNESCO World Heritage 2007) → Cheonjeyeon (Natural Monument 1993) → Jusangjeolli (Natural Monument 2005) → **Osulloc (Korea Architecture Award 2009 / designboom Top 10 art museums)**. The 16:40 arrival also catches **late-afternoon golden light on the Seogwang Dawon green-tea fields** — the most-photographed visual signature of the stop.

sources: Amorepacific Corporation — Suh Sung-whan tea reclamation history (1979)
Architectural Institute of Korea — 2009 Korea Architecture Award records
VisitJeju — Osulloc Tea Museum official listing

itineraryStops.name: Drop-off (5 points across Jeju City)
itineraryStops.description: **Drop-off mirrors the morning pickup with one extra option**: Dongmun Traditional Market → Jeju City Hall → Hagwi-dong → Jeju International Airport, with a pre-City-Hall optional stop at **Yeon-dong / Nohyeong** for guests in those hotel zones (5th drop point). Estimated arrival: Dongmun ~18:30, City Hall ~18:40, Yeon-dong ~18:45, Hagwi ~18:55, Airport ~19:05. **Same-day evening flights from JIA** typically viable (last departure ~21:30). Coach Wi-Fi available throughout. Driver coordinates with any guest-flagged shop/hotel adjustments en route.
highlights: **5 drop-off points** — Dongmun, City Hall, Yeon-dong/Nohyeong, Hagwi, Airport
**Dongmun Traditional Market** — optional dinner + tangerine product shopping
Estimated arrival: Dongmun ~18:30 → Airport ~19:05
**Same-day evening flights from JIA** typically viable (last departure ~21:30)
Coach Wi-Fi + flexible driver coordination for hotel/shop adjustments
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Dongmun Market drop-off = optional dinner stop. City Hall + Yeon-dong + Hagwi cover hotel-zone guests. Airport drop covers same-day evening departures (last flight ~21:30 from JIA = viable). 40-min total drop-off window covers all 5 points without backtracking.

routePhases.description: Eoseungsaengak Trail climb on Hallasan's lower slopes (1.3 km wooden stairs each way; tougher than Seongsan).
Camellia Hill flower garden then local Jeju lunch.
O'sulloc Tea Museum and the surrounding fields.
South coast → Jeju City via 5 drop-off points.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A southern-Jeju loop that hits the island's most photographed UNESCO and signature stops in a single value-priced day. The route balances three categories — natural heritage (Hallasan, Jusangjeolli), curated landscape (Camellia Hill, O'sulloc), and the cultural pause (Cheonjeyeon's nymph legend) — without padding the day with commission shopping stops. The 4.9/5 rating across 648 reviews reflects how tightly the itinerary is calibrated for first-time Jeju visitors.

bestFor: Active travelers comfortable with full days
Travelers who want UNESCO World Heritage stops
lessIdealFor: Travelers needing a tight same-day flight buffer

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: All admissions included — rare at this price point
items.detail: Most $40–$50 Jeju group tours exclude admissions and add ₩15,000–₩20,000 of out-of-pocket per person. This route includes Camellia Hill, Cheonjeyeon, Jusangjeolli, and O'sulloc admissions in the base price. The only on-day cost is lunch (~₩15,000).

items.label: Explicit 'no shopping' policy
items.detail: The operator publishes an explicit no-shopping clause — there are no ginseng shops, cosmetic-mall detours, or commission lunch stops. Every stop on the route is an actual attraction. The Dongmun Market drop-off is self-guided after the tour ends, not a forced stop.

items.label: Airport-friendly first pickup
items.detail: The Jeju Airport pickup at Gate 3 (3F) at 08:45 is timed for visitors arriving on the first morning flights from Seoul. You can land at 07:30, walk to the gate, and be on the tour with your luggage 75 minutes later — eliminating the typical 'arrival-day hotel check-in dead time.'

items.label: Mini-coach format, not a 40-person bus
items.detail: The shared mini-coach format keeps groups small enough that reviews consistently mention the guides personally — Steven, Chloe, Jina, Hays — by name. Larger commission-bus tours operate at 30–40 passengers; this format runs closer to 12–18, which is the sweet spot for guide attention without paying private-tour rates.

The all-admissions-included structure is unusual at this price tier.

Eoseungsaengak Trail is 1.3 km of wooden-stair climbing each way (≈50–60 min round-trip; tougher than Seongsan Ilchulbong).
Camellia Hill is large but flat with paved paths.
Cheonjeyeon has stairs to the lower viewpoints.
Jusangjeolli has boardwalk with railings.

practicalWeatherStatic._seasonalSummary: Jeju has Korea's mildest climate — coastal areas rarely freeze in winter, and summers are humid but moderated by sea breeze. The Hallasan trailhead is 5–10°C colder than coastal Jeju year-round.

Humid; afternoon showers common. Camellia Hill rotates to hydrangea bloom.
Best season — clear, mild, with pink muhly bloom at Camellia Hill in Oct–Nov.
Mild on coast; Hallasan upper slopes can have snow. Camellia Hill peak bloom.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour available year-round?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes. The route runs every day of the year. Camellia Hill rotates blooms by season (camellia winter, canola spring, hydrangea summer, pink muhly autumn), so the photographic experience changes through the year but the day's pacing stays the same.

staticQuestions.question: Do I need hiking shoes for Eoseungsaengak?
staticQuestions.answer: Comfortable closed-toe walking shoes are sufficient — no technical hiking gear required. The Eoseungsaengak Trail is 1.3 km of wooden-stair climb each way (≈50–60 min round-trip), so trekking shoes with grip are nicer if you own them but not required. The route does not include the Hallasan summit climb (which would need dedicated gear and an early-morning start).

staticQuestions.question: How is this different from the Eastern UNESCO tour?
staticQuestions.answer: The Eastern UNESCO tour (same operator) covers Seongsan Ilchulbong (Sunrise Peak), Seongeup Folk Village, Hamdeok Beach, Micheon Cave, and the Haenyeo Museum. This Southern tour covers Hallasan, Camellia Hill, Cheonjeyeon, Jusangjeolli, and O'sulloc. Most visitors with 2 Jeju days do one of each. East has UNESCO sunrise; South has waterfalls and tea fields.

staticQuestions.question: Is lunch included?
staticQuestions.answer: No — lunch is at your own expense (~₩15,000). The guide will recommend a local restaurant and assist with vegetarian, vegan, halal, or allergy needs if you advise at booking.

staticQuestions.question: Can I bring luggage on the airport pickup?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes. Notify the operator of luggage volume in advance. The mini-coach has dedicated luggage space and visitors arriving on early-morning flights can join the tour directly with their bags.

staticQuestions.question: Is there really no shopping stop?
staticQuestions.answer: Correct — the operator publishes a no-shopping clause. Other budget Jeju group tours typically include 30–60 minute stops at ginseng centers or cosmetic shops as a cost-offset for the operator. This route excludes those entirely.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to southern Jeju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: An active day with several walking-heavy stops.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for families whose kids can handle a full day of moving around.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Recommended only for active seniors comfortable with stairs and uneven ground.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is around age 10 and up — children comfortable with a full-day rhythm and the walking notes above.

pickup_dropoff.notes: AtoC Korea curated edit: Eongdeongmul Valley removed from the GetYourGuide standard route to focus on the five highest-impact stops. All admissions included; explicit no-shopping policy.

walking_notes: Active walking day with some stairs and uneven terrain.
keywords: jeju southern top unesco spots tour
synonym_hints: jeju southern top unesco spots tour

matching_metadata.audit_summary: South Jeju UNESCO + heritage signature loop covering 5 distinct UNESCO/Natural Monument layers in one day: Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage 2007 + Biosphere 2002 (Eoseungsaengak trail), Camellia Hill (largest camellia arboretum in the East, 6,000 trees / 500+ species from 80 countries, year-round bloom), Cheonjeyeon Falls (Natural Monument No. 378), Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff (Natural Monument No. 443, East Asia's premier columnar-jointing site), Osulloc Tea Museum (Korea's largest green-tea field + 2009 Korea Architecture Award). Botanically-anchored counterpart to the west-south-authentic sister tour, which emphasizes Hallasan 1100 Wetland Ramsar site + Songaksan double-crater + Hyeopjae Beach.

high_scores.unesco_anchor_count=5: 5 distinct UNESCO/Natural Monument layers in one day — densest UNESCO concentration in catalog Jeju routes
high_scores.year_round_attraction_fit=1.0: Camellia Hill's year-round bloom calendar + Osulloc indoor museum + Hallasan all-season = no seasonal-risk tour
high_scores.honeymoon_fit=1.0: Camellia + Osulloc + waterfalls = honeymoon-classic south-Jeju trifecta
high_scores.geology_enthusiast_fit=1.0: Hallasan Triple Natural designation + Jusangjeolli East Asia premier columnar-jointing
high_scores.botanical_enthusiast_fit=1.0: Catalog's only stop with the East's largest camellia arboretum
high_scores.tea_enthusiast_fit=1.0: Osulloc + Seogwang Dawon = Korea's tea-culture flagship destination

low_scores.private_tour_fit=0.05: Format is small-group fixed itinerary only
low_scores.off_beaten_path_fit=0.4: All anchors are popular tourist sites — not for crowd-avoiders

matching_metadata.differentiation_vs_west_south_authentic_: Same Jusangjeolli + Cheonjeyeon + Osulloc anchors, but **UNESCO + botanical emphasis** (Hallasan Eoseungsaengak + Camellia Hill) vs. sister's **alpine wetland + double-crater volcano + west-coast beach emphasis** (Hallasan 1100 Wetland Ramsar + Songaksan + Hyeopjae Beach). This tour = 5 UNESCO/Natural Monument anchors + year-round bloom calendar + tea-culture finale; sister = Ramsar wetland + WWII coastal tunnels + emerald-water beach sunset. Pair complementarily for guests with 2 days on Jeju.

props.headlineLine2: southern Jeju's signature loop in a value-priced day

itineraryStops.name: Pickup (4 points across Jeju City)
itineraryStops.description: Sequential pickup across four central locations. Choose the closest at booking: Ocean Suites Jeju Hotel (08:30), Jeju International Airport Gate 3, 3rd Floor Domestic Departures (08:45), LOTTE City Hotel Jeju (08:55), Shilla Duty-Free Jeju Store (09:05). Airport pickup allows visitors arriving on early-morning flights to start the tour immediately with luggage.
highlights: 4 sequential pickup points
Airport luggage-friendly pickup
Air-conditioned mini-coach
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Pickup point chosen for efficient guest assembly while preserving on-time tour start.

_poi_meta.note: Operational stop (pickup); identified by name pattern. visitBasics/convenience/smartNotes were not factsheet-derived.

itineraryStops.name: Eoseungsaengak Trail (Hallasan UNESCO)
itineraryStops.description: A scenic but demanding climb on Hallasan's lower slopes. Hallasan is South Korea's highest peak (1,947 m) and part of the Jeju Volcanic Island UNESCO World Natural Heritage and Biosphere Reserve. The Eoseungsaengak Trail is a 1.3 km wooden-stair climb (each way) to the Eoseungsaeng-oreum summit at 1,169 m, opening onto panoramic views of the Hallasan crater and the southern coast. Round-trip ≈50–60 minutes; expect a steady stair climb at the start and another near the summit — more strenuous than Seongsan Ilchulbong.
highlights: UNESCO World Heritage (Hallasan)
1.3 km wooden-stair climb each way
Panoramic Hallasan crater views
Eoseungsaeng-oreum summit (1,169 m)
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Featured anchor stop on this route — chosen for its cultural/scenic significance and fit with the day's pacing.

visitBasics.hours: Trail entry from 05:00; seasonal closures apply
visitBasics.closed: Weather-dependent (snow, fog, high wind closures common Nov–Mar)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — short but with many stairs; some steep sections

smartNotes.photo: Wide-angle shots from the upper lookout work better than close portraits; clear-day views to Jeju City
smartNotes.facilities: Best facilities at trail entrance (Eorimok); none along the route
smartNotes.tip: Best as a focused scenic walk, not a slow stroll; check weather before — high-altitude conditions change fast

itineraryStops.name: Camellia Hill (year-round signature flower garden)
itineraryStops.description: Jeju's most loved private flower garden — 6,000+ camellia trees across 250 varieties, supplemented by hydrangea (summer), pink muhly grass (autumn), and canola flowers (spring). The site has photo-zone arches, greenhouse exhibits, and the adjacent Snoopy Garden. Camellias themselves are at peak from December through February but the garden works year-round thanks to seasonal flower rotation.
highlights: 6,000+ camellia trees
250 varieties
Year-round seasonal flower rotation
Snoopy Garden adjacent
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Featured anchor stop on this route — chosen for its cultural/scenic significance and fit with the day's pacing.

visitBasics.admission: Adult 10,000 KRW / Teen 8,000 KRW / Child 7,000 KRW (approximate)
visitBasics.walking: Easy to moderate — gentle hill paths; mostly stroller-accessible

convenience.restroom: Available at entrance and within garden

smartNotes.photo: Hydrangea tunnel in June; pink muhly grass field in October; camellia varieties peak Nov–Apr
smartNotes.facilities: Indoor greenhouse café, gift shop, picnic areas
smartNotes.tip: Visit before 10am or after 4pm in peak season to avoid crowds; pair with Osulloc (10 min drive)

itineraryStops.name: Lunch (Jeju local restaurant)
itineraryStops.description: Free time at a guide-recommended Jeju restaurant. Local specialties: heuk-dwaeji (Jeju black pork) BBQ, mom-guk (mugwort soup with abalone), or jeon-bok-juk (abalone porridge). Vegetarian, vegan, halal, and allergy-sensitive options available with advance notice. Operator explicitly excludes shopping/commission stops.
highlights: Heuk-dwaeji black pork
Vegetarian/vegan/halal options
No shopping detour
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Mid-route meal break aligned with guest energy and route timing.

_poi_meta.note: Operational stop (lunch); identified by name pattern. visitBasics/convenience/smartNotes were not factsheet-derived.

itineraryStops.name: Cheonjeyeon Waterfall (Pond of the Gods)
itineraryStops.description: A three-tier waterfall in a forested volcanic valley, locally called the 'Pond of the Gods.' Legend has seven heavenly nymphs descending from the sky to bathe in the upper pool. The Seonimgyo Bridge above the gorge is decorated with carvings of the seven nymphs. Boardwalks and stairs through subtropical forest.
highlights: Three-tier waterfall
Seven Nymphs legend
Seonimgyo Bridge of Seven Nymphs
Forest boardwalk
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Featured anchor stop on this route — chosen for its cultural/scenic significance and fit with the day's pacing.

visitBasics.walking: Moderate — many stairs through the valley; not stroller-friendly; mobility-difficult

convenience.restroom: At entrance/parking area only — none past the ticket office

smartNotes.photo: The second tier from its viewing deck is the iconic 30m waterfall shot; Seonimgyo Bridge with seven nymphs is also striking
smartNotes.facilities: Small souvenir shop and café at entrance
smartNotes.tip: The first tier is dry except after heavy rain — don't be surprised if it's not flowing; allow 1.5–2 hours to see all three tiers; do NOT confuse with Cheonjiyeon Falls (different falls in Seogwipo)

itineraryStops.name: Jusangjeolli Cliff (Daepo Columnar Joints)
itineraryStops.description: Hexagonal columnar lava cliffs along Jeju's south coast — formed when Hallasan's lava flows cooled rapidly on contact with the sea. The geometric cliff face is one of Korea's most photographed coastal features and is part of the Jeju UNESCO Global Geopark. Boardwalks with railings; flat walking with sea-spray viewpoints.
highlights: Hexagonal lava columns
UNESCO Global Geopark
South-coast boardwalk
Sea-spray viewpoints
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Featured anchor stop on this route — chosen for its cultural/scenic significance and fit with the day's pacing.

visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat wooden boardwalk along cliff; total walk 15–20 min

smartNotes.photo: Best with high tide and rough seas — waves smashing into the columns make dramatic shots; golden hour gives best column-color contrast
smartNotes.facilities: Small geological exhibition near entrance; souvenir shop
smartNotes.tip: Get there before 11am or after 4pm to avoid tour-bus crowds; combine with Jungmun Saekdal Beach (5 min) for a half-day

itineraryStops.name: O'sulloc Tea Museum + Green Tea Fields
itineraryStops.description: Jeju's signature green-tea estate, founded in 1979 by the Amorepacific group. The museum traces Korean tea culture; the surrounding tea fields are an iconic Jeju photo backdrop. Free admission. The on-site café serves green tea ice cream, tea-leaf cakes, and matcha lattes. The Innisfree House next door is a small cosmetic-experience space.
highlights: Korean tea culture museum
Iconic green tea field photo backdrop
Green tea ice cream + matcha desserts
Innisfree House nearby
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Featured anchor stop on this route — chosen for its cultural/scenic significance and fit with the day's pacing.

visitBasics.admission: Free for museum; tea/desserts in café paid; Innisfree workshops paid separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat museum and stroller-friendly tea fields; well-maintained paths

smartNotes.photo: Tea fields with tour-cup shaped museum building; outdoor seating area; cherry blossoms peak shoulder season
smartNotes.facilities: Café (must-try green tea ice cream), gift shop, Innisfree Jeju House next door, observatory deck overlooking fields
smartNotes.tip: Arrive before 10:30 to beat the tour-bus rush; allow 1.5–2 hours including café time and tea field walk

itineraryStops.name: Drop-off (5 points across Jeju City)
itineraryStops.description: Five drop-off options: Shilla Duty-Free, LOTTE City Hotel, Jeju Airport, Ocean Suites Jeju, and Dongmun Traditional Market (drop-only — no shopping detour). Dongmun is Jeju's largest night market and a 5-minute walk from Heuk-dwaeji (Black Pork) Street.
highlights: 5 drop-off options
Dongmun Market self-guided drop
Black Pork Street nearby
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Return logistics — drop-off chosen for guest convenience and timing certainty.

_poi_meta.note: Operational stop (return); identified by name pattern. visitBasics/convenience/smartNotes were not factsheet-derived.

routePhases.description: Eoseungsaengak Trail climb on Hallasan's lower slopes (1.3 km wooden stairs each way; tougher than Seongsan).
Camellia Hill flower garden then local Jeju lunch.
O'sulloc Tea Museum and the surrounding fields.
South coast → Jeju City via 5 drop-off points.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A southern-Jeju loop that hits the island's most photographed UNESCO and signature stops in a single value-priced day. The route balances three categories — natural heritage (Hallasan, Jusangjeolli), curated landscape (Camellia Hill, O'sulloc), and the cultural pause (Cheonjeyeon's nymph legend) — without padding the day with commission shopping stops. The 4.9/5 rating across 648 reviews reflects how tightly the itinerary is calibrated for first-time Jeju visitors.

bestFor: Active travelers comfortable with full days
Travelers who want UNESCO World Heritage stops
lessIdealFor: Travelers needing a tight same-day flight buffer

staticQuestions.question: Is there really no shopping stop?
staticQuestions.answer: Correct — the operator publishes a no-shopping clause. Other budget Jeju group tours typically include 30–60 minute stops at ginseng centers or cosmetic shops as a cost-offset for the operator. This route excludes those entirely.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to southern Jeju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: An active day with several walking-heavy stops.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for families whose kids can handle a full day of moving around.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Recommended only for active seniors comfortable with stairs and uneven ground.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is around age 10 and up — children comfortable with a full-day rhythm and the walking notes above.

props.note: checkout_tour_id resolves at runtime from Supabase / env; not part of static JSONB.

price.priceNote: Original price 43 USD on GetYourGuide; AtoC Korea sale price is a flat 10% discount.

fix_passes.v12: P0+P1 — pickup_dropoff Schema A unification, routeFlowStops type-based, competing_products → internal slugs, anchor_poi_keys aligned
fix_passes.v13: P1.5 — pickup_dropoff Schema A.2 → A.1; practicalAccordionItems / routePhases / seasonalVariations unified to V4 canonical (no Tailwind in data)
fix_passes.v14: P2 — _publication uniform; duration formatting standardized; glanceItems Duration added where missing
fix_passes.v15: P3 round 1 — SEO trim. seo.pageTitle rewritten to ≤65 chars (was 14/25 over 70); seo.metaDescription rewritten to ≤168 chars (was 25/25 over 170). primaryKeywords and ogImage unchanged. Hero/OG images, POI verification, review imports, and routePhases enrichment deferred to later P3 rounds.
fix_passes.v15.2_charter_consolidation: Removed "jeju-popular-sightseeing-private-car-charter-tour" from competing_products per Jason's one-charter-per-region rule (jeju-island-private-car-charter-tour remains as canonical Jeju charter).

v17_schema_unification.description: Phase 1: Converted matching_profile scores from 1-5 int scale to 0-1 float scale to match the 3 Step 3 new tours. Mapping 1→0.20, 2→0.40, 3→0.60, 4→0.80, 5→1.00. Mechanical conversion preserves ordinal information; verified-fact recalibration happens in subsequent enhancement batches.

v17_batch3_enhancement.description: Batch 3 deep enhancement — descriptions raised to 700-1100+ chars; KB-backed metadata on all attraction stops; matching_profile recalibrated with verified-fact 0-1 float scores. Indoor ratio computed time-weighted using KB indoor_ratio_at_stop methodology established in Fix 1.

_score_audit._methodology: 0-1 float scale. Indoor ratio computed time-weighted from KB indoor_ratio_at_stop values per Jason's correction methodology.

unesco_fit.reasoning: Verified: 2 of 6 stops directly UNESCO — Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage (Eoseungsaengak gateway) + Daepo Jusangjeolli is on UNESCO Global Geopark list. Cheonjeyeon Falls + O'sulloc + Camellia Hill are non-UNESCO. 0.95 reflects strong UNESCO core but not pure UNESCO loop.

walking_level.reasoning: Verified totals: Eoseungsaengak 1.3 km + 169 m gain (steep), Camellia Hill ~2 km flat, Cheonjeyeon ~0.5 km + stair section, Jusangjeolli ~0.5 km flat boardwalk, O'sulloc 1 km flat. Total ~5 km + Eoseungsaengak elevation. Mechanical 0.8 was overstated; verified 0.65 with one moderate climb.

scenic_level.reasoning: Verified 6 distinct landscape types: alpine oreum summit (Eoseungsaengak), botanical color-themed garden (Camellia Hill), three-tier waterfall + nymph bridge (Cheonjeyeon), basalt columnar coast (Jusangjeolli), tea-field rolling hills (O'sulloc). High landscape diversity = 0.9.

photo_level.reasoning: Verified: 6 hero-image spots — Hallasan summit ridge, window-frame camellia (Camellia Hill signature), Seonimgyo nymph bridge over Cheonjeyeon, hexagonal basalt columns, O'sulloc tea fields. 0.95 calibrated.

iconic_landmark_fit.reasoning: Verified: 3 of 6 stops universally iconic (Hallasan, Jusangjeolli, O'sulloc); Camellia Hill regionally iconic; Cheonjeyeon Falls famous. 0.85 calibrated.

stroller_fit.reasoning: Verified: Camellia Hill flat paved (good), Jusangjeolli boardwalk (good), O'sulloc flat (good), Cheonjeyeon has steep stairs (NOT stroller-friendly), Eoseungsaengak is a 169 m climb (NOT stroller-friendly). 3/5 stroller-friendly = 0.5.

mobility_friendly_fit.reasoning: Same logic as stroller_fit — Eoseungsaengak elevation + Cheonjeyeon stairs limit access. 3/5 stops mobility-friendly = 0.55.

indoor_ratio.reasoning: MAJOR CORRECTION using time-weighted method (per Jason's methodology): Eoseungsaengak 5%, Camellia Hill 15%, Cheonjeyeon Falls 5%, Jusangjeolli 0%, O'sulloc 50%, time-weighted = 50.25/330 ≈ 0.15 excluding lunch. With lunch (80% indoor) included: 98.25/390 = 0.25. Catalog value 0.20 represents the substantive-attraction average. Mechanical Phase 1 conversion 0.6 was wildly off.

weather_sensitivity.reasoning: UPGRADED — 80% of stop time is outdoor. Eoseungsaengak summit very wind-and-weather exposed. Camellia Hill garden weather-dependent. Songaksan-style. Verified high weather sensitivity.

rain_fit.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — only O'sulloc Tea Museum interior is a reliable rain refuge. Eoseungsaengak, Cheonjeyeon, Jusangjeolli, and most of Camellia Hill are weather-exposed. Verified poor rain hedge.

intangible_heritage_fit.reasoning: Verified: only Cheonjeyeon Falls has direct intangible heritage (Chilseonyeo Festival, Seven Nymphs legend). Hallasan and Jusangjeolli are natural heritage but not intangible. O'sulloc tea ceremony has cultural significance but not registered intangible heritage. 0.55 calibrated.

first_time_fit.reasoning: Verified: covers Hallasan UNESCO + columnar geopark + iconic waterfall + tea culture + flower garden — 5 distinct Jeju essentials. 0.95 is excellent first-Jeju overview but slightly missing food/beach themes.

value_for_money_fit.reasoning: Verified fees: Camellia Hill ₩12,000 + others mostly free or ₩2,000. Total ~₩14,000 attraction fees per adult. Strong value but Camellia Hill is the cost driver. 0.85 calibrated.

young_kids_fit.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — Eoseungsaengak 169 m climb taxing for under-7s; Cheonjeyeon stairs same issue. Camellia Hill, Jusangjeolli, O'sulloc all kid-friendly. 3/5 kid-friendly + 1 lunch = 0.55. Mechanical 0.8 overstated.

senior_fit.reasoning: Slight downgrade — Eoseungsaengak climb + Cheonjeyeon stairs excludes mobility-impaired seniors. Active seniors fine; general seniors face real obstacles. 0.55 verified.

culture_level.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — verified: 1 strong cultural anchor (O'sulloc tea museum + Korean tea heritage) + Cheonjeyeon legend. Most of the route is nature/garden. 0.65 calibrated.
